Angela Erdmann did not see her father’s father. He was dead in 1946, six years before her birth. But, on Tuesday 8 April, 2014, she gave an account of the special time when she got a note in a bottle, 101 years after her father’s father put it in the sea by the land of the Baltic. This note is said to be the oldest one of its sort in the world. It was given to Erdmann by the house for things from past times which now has it for public view in Germany.

“It was a great surprise,” Angela Erdmann, 62, said, saying again how she got news of the bottle. “A man came to my door and gave word that he had post from my father’s father. He then said that a note in a bottle had been seen and that the name on the card was that of my father’s father.” 

Her friend was a knowledge worker for families who had made a search for her in Berlin after the letter was given to the building for sea history in the north town of Hamburg. The brown bottle for beer, which had been in water for 101 years, was seen in the fishing net of Konrad Fischer, a man who fishes, who had gone out into the sea by the north town of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, chief for sea and science at the house for things from past times, said this bottle note was the oldest one he had come across. “There are papers which have been seen without bottles which are older and are in the house for things from past times,” he said. “But, with the bottle and the paper, this is for certain the oldest we have now. It is in very good condition.”

Police and government experts in countries over the world have, for almost 100 years, used lie machines to help make criminals responsible or to make secret agents and persons against their country come to light. The lie machine is loved in motion pictures, with many serious events showing the person who did the crime with wet face and body as they are joined to the machine. But the machine may not be in use for much longer. Experts in Britain and the Netherlands have made important new discovery, making a way with more than 70% good results in tests. This way may be in police buildings in countries over the world in ten years.

Instead of using face movements, much talking or moving arms—which are all seen as signs of lies—the new way is to look at motions of the whole body to give a sign if a person has thoughts of being guilty. The lie machine is used a lot in America by police and others and for government work in the FBI and CIA but is not used much in Europe. Many in science and law have doubts about how good it is. But, the new way from these experts has made good results in tests.

The simple idea is that persons who say things that are not true make more small moves, so using a special all-body motion covering—the same kind movies use to make living pictures—will give this knowledge. One expert of the group, Ross Anderson, said, “Many years of work on lies show that a questioner is only a little better than chance at seeing who is telling the truth.” He said the new way, by comparison, got more than 70% right. In some tests, the group already got more than 80%. Anderson said, “The important idea is that persons with guilty thoughts move more and we are able to make a strong measure of this.”

The lie machine was made in 1921 by police worker John Larson, who used ideas from mind expert William Marston. It keeps a record of changes in heart movement, blood force, wet skin, and breath to see if a person is not saying true things. Motion pictures make it seem as if the machine is almost always right, but in 1998 the highest law group in America said there was no agreement that it was safe, and the group of important American science experts said the same thing in 2003.

Do you want your child to be good at sports, make the school team and, maybe one day, even take part in a great world event? Well, try to make certain your future winner is given birth in November or, if not, in October. A test has given the idea that school children born in these months are in better condition than others in their group. Children with November and October birth days came out fitter, stronger and more full of power than those born in any of the other ten months, most of all when compared to children born in April or June.

Dr. Gavin Sandercock, who is from the Centre for Sports and Exercise Science at Essex University and was head of the test, saw that children with autumn birth days had a clear physical help over others in their group. The study took in 8,550 boys and girls aged between ten and sixteen from 26 public schools in Essex. All were given three different tests between 2007 and 2010 to measure fitness — lasting power, hand power, and lower-body power.

The outcome made clear that the month when a child is born may make important changes to how strong their heart and muscles are, as well as how quickly they can go, all of which are important for sports, where such qualities are needed. The results from sports days in schools in the coming weeks may give support to these discoveries. Children born in November had the best results because they had the most lasting power and full power, and were second in strength. Those born in October were almost as good, having the highest marks for strength and coming third for power, with December children not far behind.

The difference in body power between children in the same group with different birth months was sometimes very great. For example, a boy born in November may run 10% quicker, jump 12% higher and have 15% more power than a child the same age who was born in April. This may give a very great help, said Sandercock. Such differences might, in the end, be the reason why some become top sportsmen, because selection for the best sports groups may sometimes be decided by very small changes in physical acts.

The study, which is in the International Paper for Sports Medicine, saw that when the three fitness scores were put together, those born in April were the least fit, with June next. Because of this, these children might not be taken into school teams and may do less well at sports, said Sandercock.

The small room is made to be like a school-room. Its walls of iron with lines are covered with teaching pictures – letters of the alphabet with pictures and a map of Bangladesh. But there is a loud sound of hammering again and again, and there are chemicals in the air which make the back part of the mouth and the eyes unhappy, so it is hard to give attention. The boys and girls who get learning in this room, three meters square, are the ones with good chance. They are not working in the factories across the road. For 14 years, SOHAY, a group which is not part of the government, has been working with help from the Global Fund for Children and Comic Relief, in poor parts of Dhaka to get working boys and girls into school. It gives attention to children working in dangerous places – in factories for making aluminum and plastic, and in places where leather is made. The school-room is one of 23 places in the town which SOHAY has started. These places get children ready for first school with learning to make their knowledge better. When they are at first school, the boys and girls may do their school-work at the centers, with help from other boys and girls. Alamin, ten, who was working in a plastic factory before, comes to one of the centers. His father is a seller in the street and his mother has work in houses part of the time. They are all happy because he is now at school and not doing dangerous work. His friend Rabi says he has a desire to let his past in the factory go. “I like school,” he says. SOHAY has meetings for fathers, mothers, and those who give work to boys and girls, to give them the idea that children should not work, and gives learning for new work so families have more money. “Getting working boys and girls into regular schools is very hard work," says SOHAY’s chief, Mohammed al-Mamun. “Their minds and bodies are not the same as other boys and girls in society. After work, they sometimes have trouble making friends and joining in at school.” It is very hard, also, to make certain they keep going to school – the number of children who stop is very high. Zhorna Akter Sumayya, who is seven, has two older brothers, both working (one in a food place, one in a club). But after she was given a start with learning in one of SOHAY’s places, now she goes to

A wide and important argument between countries has started about the right of native Indian groups in Bolivia to put coca leaves in their mouths and crush them with their teeth. These leaves are the main substance used to make cocaine. Bolivia has got a special approval to be free from the 1961 Agreement about strong drugs, which is the book of rules that has control over drug laws between countries. This approval lets its native people have the right to use coca leaves in this way. Bolivia said the agreement is not in harmony with its new book of laws, made in 2009, which gives a rule to “keep old and native coca as part of its history” and says that coca “in its normal form ... is not a dangerous drug.” South American native people have been putting coca leaves in their mouths for a very long time. These leaves are said to give strong feeling in the body and to have good effect for health. Friends of Bolivia’s position said good things about Bolivia for keeping the rights of native people safe. “What Bolivia did gives hope and is a new way,” said Danny Kushlick, Head of Outside Work at the Transform Drug Policy Group, which works for changing drug laws. “This is a sign that any nation that has had enough of the drug war can make changes to its agreement with the rules of the UN.” But the UN’s International Drug Control Group, which has the work of watching the use of the world drug agreements, said Bolivia is putting the world control of drugs in danger. Some countries – like the UK, the US, Italy, Sweden, the Netherlands, and Russia – were against the request from Bolivia. The UK’s words to the UN, which has the work of watching over the Agreement, said “The United Kingdom sees and gives respect to the fact that coca leaf is important in the culture of Bolivia,” but added: “The United Kingdom is … troubled because this special approval might make more coca be produced and – most of all – bring more coca to be used to make cocaine. Because of this, the special approval may make world law less strong when it comes to the fight against illegal drug business and may make the answer of all countries together to the drug business less strong.”

On Black Fell, looking down on the beautiful Kielder Water store of Northumberland, a group of persons are waiting in a car park by a strange wood building with a simple design from the future. This is Kielder Star House, the center of Britain’s new star-travel business. These who are waiting outside are the lucky ones. More persons had made a request for a night at the star house, but not more persons are let in. Inside, near a wood fire and under low lights, the first and chief star expert, Gary Fildes, who was a brick worker and is a man with long hair, gives a talk to his work friends. The group talks about the chance of seeing the north lights but Fildes is not certain. So, they make a decision to put their strong star glasses on Jupiter and Venus and later to look at stars like Capella and Betelgeuse. There is also the chance to see the International Space Station. “Remember,” Fildes says to his group, “it is about working together, it is about giving pleasure, it is about giving persons new ideas.” Fildes, 49, is at the front of this growing business for seeing stars in the UK. The most important time for Northumberland came in 2013 when all the national park with Hadrian’s Wall, Kielder Water and Forest Park, about 1,500 square kilometers, was given Dark Sky Park name, the only one in England. These parks are not common. The 2013 Star Count gave the fact that only 5% of UK persons are able to see more than 31 stars on a good night. The International Dark-Sky Authority in Tucson, Arizona, gives the name of Dark Sky Park only to places that take strong steps to keep out light waste. These places must also give proof that their night sky is dark enough. In Northumberland Dark Sky Park, as the place is now named, it is so dark that Venus makes a shade on the earth. Duncan Wise, visitor development head for the Northumberland National Park Authority, helped to lead the work for the dark-sky name. “We give our attention to land as everything to the line of the earth,” Wise said. “But what about what is over it?” Wise and other persons took years making their paper for the Authority, taking much light measurement and joining a group of local rulers, park groups, and persons in the town to make a light plan for outside, which gives a guide for the building

When Larry Pizzi first got news about electric bicycles almost 20 years ago, he said: “Why would any person put a motor and batteries on a bicycle?” This is a question which still seems strange to persons who like the old way. Shops selling bicycles have been slow to have electric bicycles, even though these bicycles have been here from the end of the 1990s. Sales in the US have not been great. Pizzi, who is now head of Currie Technologies, the top seller of electric bicycles in the US, has the idea this will be different soon. Others in the business say the same. Well-known names like Trek, Raleigh and Specialized all have electric sorts and are putting their hope in the market getting much better. “We are almost at the point where most persons will be using electric bicycles,” Pizzi said. “There are more persons coming into this group, it seems, with every month.” The US is different from other countries when it is about electric bicycles. Almost 32 million electric bicycles were sold in 2014, nearly all in China, where they are most times used for transport. Electric bicycles are liked in much of Europe, too. They are normal in the Netherlands and Switzerland; workers for the German post use them to get from place to place and BMW has one for about $3,000. Electric bicycles are not the same as motor-bicycles or mopeds, which get their power from a motor; they are bicycles which you can put forward by pushing with your feet – with or without help from an electric motor. Being on an electric bicycle is like being on a normal bicycle with a strong wind at your back; the motor gives you help to go quicker or go up hills but is not the chief cause moving the bicycle. Electric bicycles, not like mopeds, are normally given approval to be on bicycle roads and they may not go quicker than 20 miles in one hour.

This is the condition of the present-day chemical expert: you wait a long time for a new substance to come, and then four come together. Experts in Japan, Russia and the US have made the discovery. These four new substances are the first to be put on the table of elements since 2011, when substances 114 and 116 were named. All these new substances are very short-lived and very full of radiation. They make the seventh line of the table of elements complete, and make science books everywhere old.

The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), a group in the US which gives names and rules for chemical words, measurement and such, said the new substances were true on 30 December, 2015, after looking at work going back to 2004. The experts who made the discovery now have to give them new names, to take the place of long Latin names – ununtrium, ununpentium, ununseptium and ununoctium – which give their numbers, 113, 115, 117, and 118. The number shows how many parts called protons are in the center of the substance.

IUPAC said that a group of Russian and American experts at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California had enough proof to say they had made substances 115, 117 and 118. The group gave the reward for finding 113, which was also looked for by the Russian and American experts, to a team at the RIKEN Institute in Japan. This decision lets Japan be the first country in Asia to give a name to an element. By IUPAC rules, new substances may take names from old stories, stones, a place or country, or a science expert.

Putting out a car with a top speed of 25 miles for every hour, two seats and no foot-pedals or controlling wheel may not make much of an imprint at a motor show. But Google, in America, sent a small shock through the car and taxi business when it put out the newest form of its no-driver car. The car, working by electric power, which Google has started to test near its main building in Mountain View, California, has no common controls, not even foot-pedals. In its place, there is an instrument on your hand that lets you give the car orders and tell it where to go, and a single STOP button between the two front seats for stopping the car if needed. The car, in fact, takes over all the work of finding the way, turning, moving faster, and stopping. The company is making about 100 test-cars for a two-year test. The company’s chief, Sergey Brin, said at a meeting in California that the car was “still at the test stage” but that the purpose was “about changing the existence for men and women who are not well helped by transport today.” About the car, he said: “You are only sitting; no steering wheel, no foot-pedals. For me, it was very pleasing. About ten seconds after coming in, I was not conscious I was even there. It made me think of taking a chair that goes up a mountain by itself – a little bit of being alone that was very good for me.” Google says the chief purpose of the work is to make safety better, and because the car is made with soft material at the front and a plastic window, “it may be much safer than any other car for men and women walking.” The cars, which have been made for Google by a company (not named yet) in Detroit, will be used to see more about the best way to make no-driver cars work. Google will now do more tests for the cars, which are not for sale yet.

Galina Zaglumyonova was made awake in her room in the middle of Chelyabinsk by a very great explosion that sent in the balcony windows and gave her clay pots holding her small number of house plants a sudden end. When she got out of bed she saw a great white line in the morning sky and could hear car alarms from the street. "I did not get what was happening," she said. "There was a great explosion and then some small explosions. My first thought was that an air-plane had come down." What she saw was in fact the end of a ten-ton stone from space that came down to earth in fire and light after the sun came up. Rulers said that almost 1,200 persons were hurt, with more than 40 taken to hospital -- most because of bits of broken glass sent flying by the loud noise made when the stone came down. There were no dead persons. The stone came into the air at a rate of at least 33,000 miles every hour and came into bits between 18 and 32 miles above the earth, as said in words from the Russian experts. This gave fear in Chelyabinsk, a Russian town with one million persons south of the Ural mountains, as phone systems were quickly full because so many were making calls. Moving picture records taken by persons in the place, often with angry and bad words from those who saw the event, showed the bits of stone burning in the sky as they came near to the ground. The white trail was seen for hundreds of miles about, even in the country by Chelyabinsk, Kazakhstan. Tatyana Bets was at work in the waiting part of a hospital in the middle of the town when the stone came down. "First we saw the wind, then the room had a very bright light, and we saw a cloud of smoke in the sky," she said. Then, in a short time, there were explosions. At least three holes were seen later, said the persons in authority, and the army was watching them. One was more than six meters across, and another bit of the stone made a hole in the thick ice of a near water. Radiation was normal at the places where the stone came down, the army said.

An octopus has made a bold escape from the National Aquarium in New Zealand by getting out of his water store, moving down a 50-meter water pipe, and going into the sea. Like in the story of Finding Nemo, Inky – a common New Zealand octopus – made his run for freedom after the top of his water store was by chance left a little open. Workers say that in the middle of the night, when the place was empty, Inky went up to the top of his glass box, down the side of the water store, and across the floor of the building. Rob Yarrell, chief of the National Aquarium in Napier, said: “Octopuses are well known for getting away. I do not think he was sad or by himself, as octopuses are alone living animals. But he is a very curious boy. He would want to see what is outside. That is just Inky.” 

One idea is that Inky went over the floor – a way of three or four meters – and then, feeling freedom near, into a pipe that went right to the sea. The pipe was 50 meters long and ended at the water of Hawke’s Bay, on the east side of North Island, New Zealand. Another way Inky may have gone is by getting into an open pipe at the top of his water store, which went under the floor to the drain. “When we came in the morning and his water store was empty, I was very surprised,” said Yarrell, who has not started looking for Inky. “The workers and I have been quite sad. But this is Inky, and he is always a bit of a surprising animal.”

Reiss Jenkinson, keeper at the National Aquarium, said he was quite certain Inky had not been taken. “I know octopus behaviour very well,” he said. “I have seen octopuses on boats go through small water pumps. And, the care here is too strong for anyone to take Inky—and why would they?” Because octopuses have no bones, they are able to go into very small places and have been seen going through holes as small as coins. They are also thought to be very wise and able to use things as tools. 

At the Island Bay Sea Teaching Place in Wellington, an octopus was seen going to another water store at night to take crabs, then going back to its own. Another one there, Ozymandias, was thought to have made a world mark for

Hard, full of body-building material, and to be found under a stone near you. Insects have for a long time not been seen as food in most places in the world. But now they are coming nearer and nearer to our tables. In the spring of 2013, there will be an attempt to take away the feeling of disgust and put insects not only on special food lists but also in food stores. In April, there will be a meeting in London named Pestival 2013, a Wellcome Trust event for giving value to insects, where the eating of insects will be an important point. There will be a two-day short-time restaurant by the Nordic Food Lab, the group from the Danish restaurant Noma, which put ants on the table for ten days at Claridge's hotel in London in 2012. In March, a television story by the BBC will have the food writer Stefan Gates looking for and eating cooked grasshoppers and cooked spiders. But under all the attention and jokes about insects in the soup, there is a serious thought. A number of experts say there is a clear good effect for the earth if men take insects for food. The United Nations Food and Agriculture group has been giving money for work from 2011 to give help to eating and farming insects in south-east Asia and Africa, where two thousand million persons are said to take insects and caterpillar young as a normal part of their food. In 2012, the group gave out a list of 1,909 sorts of insect which are good for food, and, with help from the Dutch government, will have a great international meeting about “this important food” in 2013.

In the next thirty years, the number of persons living on earth will go up to nine thousand million. Even now, one thousand million men do not get enough food. The increase will give more trouble for farm land, water, woods, sea work, living plant and animal sorts, and also food salts and power. The price of meat is going up, not only in money but in the amount of rainwood destroyed for animals to eat, or to make food for cows. There is the further question of air waste sent out by these animals. The work of getting food from farm animals, in the way of sending bad things into the air, is very great—35% of the earth’s air waste of one sort, 65% of another, and 9% of the last sort.

The regular eight hours in the office is at an end. The most important work of the day is done; work which is not done may be kept for the morning. This is the time when a great number of workers would have thoughts of going to the door. But for millions of Japanese workers, the idea of cleaning their tables and being at home in time for food is enough for other persons to say they have no love for their company. Still, after years of giving companies full control to get all the work possible from their workers, a new question of Japan’s fixed way of overwork has come from the government, which is looking at making a new rule: workers must take at least five days of paid rest every year.

Japanese workers may now have, on average, 18.5 days of paid rest in the year—only two less than the world’s average—with a least number of ten days, and also 15 one-day public rest days. In fact, only a small number take all of these days, using only nine of their 18.5-day right. Where British workers see a two-week summer rest as a right, Japanese workers have the idea that a four-night rest is a great pleasure, almost too much.

This new step, now being talked about in meetings of the government, comes after companies started saying workers should take short sleeps at work to do better work. By the end of the coming ten years, the government hopes that if this is made law, Japanese workers will do more like British workers, who take 20 days of paid rest every year, or French workers, who take 25.

Japan’s hard work way may have given it great money power, and workers are respected in other countries for their strong support of the company, but this is sometimes at the cost of all other things. Japan has few births, and the number of persons is going down quickly, in part because men and women do not have enough time to make families. More workers are getting ill from trouble, or even worse, dying from working too much. Though tests give the idea that working long hours at the office or in the factory does not give more good work, today’s workers are still feeling bad effects together from the money boom in the 1980s.

A sad old man living in a hole on the moon is the strange chief part of John Lewis’s Christmas 2015 advertisement, as the store gives a good cause a place in its newest great-money public statement. With more talk about John Lewis’s Christmas advertisement, which is now the start of Christmas shopping time for many, the store will try to use this interest to get much money for the charity Age UK. It will also make workers and persons who buy in the store join with their nearest part of the charity to take care of old persons who may be by themselves at Christmas time. 

The store has used £7m for a group of things from the smooth TV advertisement to a phone game and goods, with pyjamas which give light in the dark, and places covered to look like the face of the moon in 11 stores. After two years of good advertisements with soft animals – a bear and a hare, and after that a small bird – this time the store is making us feel with a story of a young girl, Lily, who sees an old man living in a small house on the moon through her looking glass. The strong-willed child tries to send him a letter and sends a note by bow and arrow, before she lets a present of a looking glass go to him with a number of balloons, which in the end lets them get in touch. 

The advertisement's chief words are: “Let someone see love this Christmas,” which is almost the same as Age UK’s own words: “No one should have no one at Christmas.” Money from three things – a cup, small paper for presents, and card – will go to the charity. “The charity is in the mind of persons at this time of year and the advertisement makes us give thought to someone living near us who may not see any person,” said Rachel Swift, head of marketing at John Lewis. The advertisement has the Oasis song Half the World Away sung again by a not well known young Norwegian music-maker Aurora. The advertisement cost £1m to make. The moon pictures were made at Warner Bros Buildings, where the Harry Potter motion pictures were made, and the special place was made by one of the group behind the newest Star Wars motion picture, The Force Awakens. As in past years, John Lewis has made persons interested in their newest advertisement before its start with a small advertisement on TV and social media using the sign #OnTheMoon. There will be a full moon on Christmas Day 2015 – which is only chance, said Swift.

Nose spray with the “love substance” oxytocin may give help to young persons with autism to do better in social conditions, say experts in America. Pictures of the brains of young persons with autism disorder showed that one bit of oxytocin made the brain answer in a better way to faces. This change may make meeting with other persons seem more normal and give more pleasure for them. The experts who made this work said that a number of oxytocin uses may make the work of behaviour teaching for persons with autism more good.

“In time, one would see more right answers in social ways, more interest in being with other persons, more looking at eyes, and better talk skills,” said Kevin Pelphrey, head of the Child Brain Science Group at Yale. Autism is an early development disorder seen in more than one in every 100 persons. This condition is different in every person, but is marked by trouble in social meeting and talking. Now, there is no working treatment for the social troubles from autism.

Workers at Yale have been looking at oxytocin in the brain as a possible help for the social limits from autism because it has an important part in making connections and belief. The outcome has not been clear: one new test gave no clear help for young persons using the substance over many days. But Pelphrey said that oxytocin may help the brain to get new knowledge from meeting with others; it may do best when used with teaching that gives support for persons with autism to take part more in social ways.

“Our work makes it clear that oxytocin has an effect on the brain and gives hope that, when put with behaviour teaching, it works as a helper for social ways,” he said.

The experts used a way named functional MRI to take pictures of the brains of 17 young persons from 8 to 16 years with autism as they looked at pictures of cars or the eyes of persons with different feelings. The brain pictures were taken 45 minutes after the persons used a nose spray with nothing or oxytocin. The images showed that the part of the brain giving reward was more normal after oxytocin, showing more working when looking at faces and less when looking at the cars.

This work is in the new copy of the book Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences in America.

Benjamin Carle is 96.9% made in France, to his underpants and socks. Six forks from a store from another country, a guitar from China, and paint for the wall with no name on it kept him from being 100% a money friend of his country, but no person is complete. Carle, 26, started in 2013 to see if it was possible to live for ten months using only things made in France, for a television story. The idea came when the man for money work, Arnaud Montebourg, said people should get French things to give help to the work of making things in the country. The test made Carle do without his telephone, television, and cold-box (all from China); his glasses (Italy); his underpants (Morocco); morning drink (Guatemala) and his loved David Bowie music (England). But his long-suffering woman friend, Anaïs, and cat, Loon, (both French) kept with him.

“Men who make laws say all sorts of things and hope we will do what they say. I wanted to see if it was possible and if a person could do what the man was saying; to see if his words had value,” Carle said to the Guardian with a drink not from France in a café in Paris after his story was done.

He gave himself three rules: take only food made in France, have no touch with things made outside France, and do this with €1,800 a month (more than the lowest pay of €1,430 so he could give money for the high price of living in Paris). The news writer was surprised at the start to see only 4.5% of the things in his room were made in his own country – and the rest had to be taken out, even the light for the room (China) and green beans (Kenya). The men who took his things made Carle’s house almost without anything.

Without a cold-box (none made in France) or nail cutters, he had to put his food out on the window to make it cold and used a small knife to cut his nails. His clothing from other countries, down to his underpants, was changed for more dear things: underpants from France (€26), socks (€9), shirt with buttons (€75), shoes with cloth top (€26), but no trousers of a certain sort as none are made in France.

Through the test, Carle went through food stores for French things only, learned to

The city of Ouarzazate in Morocco is used for great works. It is on the edge of the Sahara sand and at the middle of Morocco’s movie business. Now, this trading city is the place for four joined together big sun-power stations. Together with water-power and wind, these stations will help give almost half of Morocco’s electric power from new sources by 2020, with hope that some extra may be sent to Europe. This work is important for Morocco’s purpose to use its sands and become a world sun-power nation. When all the stations are done, it will be the biggest sun-power station of its sort in the world. The first part, named Noor 1, will be working in November 2015. The mirror way used here is less common and more dear than the sun-power boards seen on many roofs, but it will keep making power even when the sun is down.

As workmen make the last changes to Noor 1, its 500,000 moon-shape mirrors shine across the sand sky. Eight hundred lines of mirrors go after the sun, turning slowly every short time as their shade moves more to the east. When all is done, the four stations at Ouarzazate will take up a space as wide as the capital city, Rabat, and make 580 units of electric power, enough for a million houses.

The chance for sun-power from the sand has been known for a long time. After the trouble at the Chernobyl atomic station in 1986, a German science man named Gerhard Knies said the world’s sands get enough heat from the sun in some hours to give all the power needed by men for a year. The hard part has been to take that power and send it to the places where most people are. Experts say sun-power will make one part of every three of Morocco’s new power by 2020, with wind and water-power being the same. “We are very happy about this work,” said Hakima el-Haite, Morocco’s head of environment. “I say it is the most important sun station in the world.”

There are more tigers in Nepal now than at any time from the 1970s. A new government list has given help to experts in animal care because the number of these great cats, which has been going down in south Asia for 100 years, may now go up. The number of wild tigers in Nepal is now 198, which is a 63.6% increase in five years, as the government has said. Maheshwar Dhakal, a plant and animal expert with Nepal’s department for national parks and animal care, said, “This is very good.” The number is from looking at pictures from more than 500 cameras in five safe places and three animal roads. More than 250 animal care workers and experts took part in the work, which cost about £250,000.

As life has become better in Asia, the price for tiger skins and body parts used in old Chinese medicine has gone up. Men from other countries pay poor Nepali men much money to take the lives of the big cats. The skins and bones are given to other men, who go through the open edge of Nepal and India, where the main traders live. One great trouble is that some men in high offices work together with bad men in animal trade.

Animal experts say the rise in tiger numbers is because of good work by police in parks, strong drives against the taking of tigers and better care of tiger living places. In Nepal, trees are on 29% of the land. But they say there is still work to do for the safety of tiger living places and the animals tigers take as food, so the big cats have enough space and food.

As the number of tigers has gone up, there are more troubles with village people. In 2012, seven persons were killed by tigers near national parks, while there were four in 2011, park men said. Village people are asking for better safety.

Robert Myslajek comes to a stop. Between two wolf foot marks in the soft earth of a mountain road, the man of science gets what he is looking for. "Wolf waste!" he says with pleasure. To see a wolf is so strange that to see their waste is a good day, even for a person with much experience. But now it is less hard. There are now about 1,500 wolves in Poland. The number has become two times more in 15 years. The wolves are – with the brown bear, the wild cat, and the fierce animal named wolverine – the last great meat-eating animals of Europe. Persons working to keep animals safe from Britain, Germany, and the Netherlands come here to see how Poland has kept this safe animal, which is even spoken badly of in old stories. The man of science, who is well known on Polish television, says wolves are special animals and can go as far as 30 kilometres on one hunt. "The Beskidy group is a strong unit, eight or nine together. This year, we have on record five young, two from last year, and two old members. We keep an eye on them with cameras in the woods that take pictures when they see motion and by going after their foot marks in the earth and snow. In every group, only one male and one female have young, only one time every year. All members take care of the young together with care and love."

Myslajek, whose father kept sheep, does not see why wolves are thought of as bad. "Why does one say 'lone-wolf gunman'? Why did we need the story of Red Riding Hood, with its bad wolf?" He has great interest in these distant dog-like animals who stayed living free 33,000 years ago when others went with man to be house dogs. Wolves are not house dogs. Myslajek says only facts of science – the need to give new life to woods and have control of wild animals – can keep Europe's wild meat-eaters safe, most of all the unliked wolf. "Natural meat-eaters keep order in nature. They keep plant-eating animals in balance, and so young trees have the chance to become high, giving resting place for birds." The rule stopping men from hunting wolves in the west Carpathian mountains was put in place in 1995 and in all Poland in 1998. There are now groups living in almost every great wood of the country. These animals live with

Unlawful downloading is a sort of bad act and theft, as much as putting one’s hand in another’s pocket and taking their money-bag is theft, says writer Philip Pullman. In an writing for Index on Censorship, Pullman, who is head of the Society of Writers, gives a strong support of copyright law. He has hard words for Internet users who say it is good to take music or books from the net without payment. “The great knowledge is so bright that persons do not see the bad thing they do,” he says. “It is shocking that any person may take an artist’s work and not get punishment. It is theft, as truly as putting your hand in another’s pocket and taking their money-bag is theft.”

His writing comes after music company heads met British government head David Cameron at Downing Street, where the trouble of web stealing was talked about. Pullman, writer of the His Dark Materials stories, says writers and music-makers do their work in poor and public-unknown places for a long time to get their work to the stage “which gives pleasure to their public and, as soon as they do this, the chance of living from this work is taken from them.” He says: “The rule is simple, and does not change because of new machine, science, or tricks: if we want to have the pleasure of a person’s work, we are to give payment for it.”

Pullman does this writing in the new number of the campaign group’s paper in a talk with Cathy Casserly, head of Creative Commons, which gives open rights to use work “so makers may keep rights in their own hands.” Casserly says that copyright has much wrong with it, and was made “in a time before new machines.” Casserly says: “By starting-point, copyright keeps persons from many ways of joining work, building more, and putting together new things from each other’s work—things which could not be thought of when these laws were made.” She says makers are to have new ideas about how they give out and get money for their work, using the words of story writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My trouble is not stealing, it is not being seen.”

From all parts of Rwanda, and even some places in the country near it, Burundi, people come to the south town of Butare to a small store named Inzozi Nziza (Sweet Dreams). They come for a taste of something new, a thing almost no one has had before—the sweet, cold, soft touch of ice cream. Here, at the first ice cream store in this middle African country, people may have cups of sweet cream, passion fruit, strawberry, and pineapple ice cream. On top, they may put new fruit, honey, small bits of chocolate, and grain mix. Black tea and coffee are for sale too. The store, which has “ice cream, coffee, dreams” on its sign, is using the interest that local people have for this food—and, at the same time, is “changing lives,” the store’s manager, Louise Ingabire, says. “Ice cream is important,” she says as she eats some with honey taste. “Some people in Rwanda like ice cream, but it is a new thing. There is still work for us, to give the news to others that they will get pleasure from it.” Like Inzozi Nziza’s words, the store may truly make dreams come true. “I did not have work before: I only kept at home. Now, I have a hope for days to come. I am making money and I am able to give some to my family,” the 27-year-old says. Butare, which has 89,600 people and is 135 kilometers south of the capital, Kigali, is where the National University of Rwanda is. Inzozi Nziza is now a meeting place for tired students who want to have something cold and new. “It is something joining people here,” Kalisa Migendo, a 24-year-old farm student, says. “If you have a need to go out and talk with a friend, a girl or a boy, you go to Inzozi Nziza for ice cream.” Most things needed for the ice cream come from near the town and the milk comes from a store in Nyanza, which is not far. The vanilla plant seeds and the soft brown powder are from other countries.

They give him the name Robin Hood of the banks. He is a man who took out a great number of loans, almost half a million euros in all, with no thought of ever giving the money back. Enric Duran gave the money to groups making and pushing ideas against the common money system. After fourteen months in a secret place, Duran is not sorry, even if what he did may put him in prison. “I am pleased with what I did,” he said in a talk by Skype from a place he did not give the name of. From 2006 to 2008, Duran took out sixty-eight business and personal loans from thirty-nine banks in Spain. He sent the money to persons working for society, giving support for talks against the money system and giving TV cameras to a news group. “I saw that, on one side, these social groups were making new systems but did not have enough money or ways to send news,” he said. “At the same time, our economy was going up without end, which made a system where money came from nothing.” The loans Duran got from banks were his answer to this condition, he said. He did not go fast at first. “I put in some papers for getting credit with my true details. They said no, but I only wanted to see what they were looking for.” After this, the man who was once a table-tennis teacher started to make a complex system of accounts, payments, and sending money. “I was learning all the time.” By the summer of 2007, he had worked out how to make the system do what he wanted, asking for loans under the name of a not-true television company. “Then, I did well and got a great amount.” Four hundred and ninety-two thousand euros, to be right.

Material from the wisdom tooth of a European man living by getting food from animals and plants has given science a new look at the sort of man who was living before farming was used. This man, living in Spain about 7,000 years back, had a strange mix of blue eyes, black or brown hair, and dark skin, from the test of his family line. He was not able to have milk and had more trouble with food from grain than the men who changed food and ways of living when they made use of new ways of working the land. When farming was made, men and animals came together in a new way and men probably got stronger in fighting disease, needing better health because animals gave them more diseases. But science may have given too much thought to the idea that farming made big changes to the way men keep healthy, because tests on the family line of this man give news that he had changes in his body making him better at fighting many bad diseases. Some still live in men of Europe today.

“Before we did this work, I had some thoughts about what we would see,” said Carles Lalueza-Fox, head of this work at the house of learning about the ways men came to be in Barcelona. “Most of these thoughts were not right.”

The group in Spain started after men going deep into holes in high ground in the north-west of Spain, in 2006, came on two bodies. These bodies, from two men about 30 years old, had been kept very well by the cold air in the hole. Tests with carbon said the bodies were about 7,000 years old, before men started farming in Europe from the east. The time was right together with old things found there, such as teeth from reindeer with holes in them for a cord, used as ornament on the clothing of these men.

Agios Efstratios is so far away, so without help from the banks, the government, and most of the new world that the mobile phone system is not able to work with numbers and there is not one money machine or card machine on the island. Before Greece had bad money trouble, the people in this quiet place in the north of the Aegean did quite well. They did their bank work at the post office and the few rooms for rent were full every summer with people who had heard, by word of mouth, about the beautiful empty beaches, clear sea, and new fish food. But, because the island still uses money in the hand, the stop of banks everywhere has been very bad. People have had to make a trip for nine hours to the nearest big island to get money, and Greek visitors say they are not able to get enough money to come. “Tourist numbers are down 80% this year,” said Mayor Maria Kakali, in an office in the village where she was a girl, a place with about 200 people. “Even people born here and living in Athens, who have their own houses on the island, are not coming.” Kakali has kept asking the government and a big Greek bank, and now they say there will be a money machine in a few weeks but she still thinks it might be too late for this summer on an island where tourism is much greater than the two other ways of living: getting fish and farming. “We have almost no orders for August, when at other times people call us again and again to get a room and we are not able to give help.” A hard winter coming may be a little better because of 50 workers living in the village to make the harbor bigger, but there is a greater trouble coming because the government has said it will stop a tax help for islands which has been there for many years.

There are almost 50,000 secret shopper journeys done every month in the United Kingdom, by the account of the Mystery Shopping Providers group. Because more money is used online, the need for secret shoppers is increasing. Secret shoppers make a false act of being a customer in a store, but their purpose is to get knowledge for how good or bad the service is.

“Store owners are seeing more and more that if people come into a building to buy something, they want a special service they do not get online,” says Simon Boydell, a representative of Marketforce, which has more than 300,000 secret shoppers on its list. “Our business friends want to see how good their stores are doing with this special feeling.”

“We give each secret shopper a number of different store places and make a change so they do not go back to the same shop in three months,” says Jill Spencer of the secret shopping business ABa. “Most days, they give almost eight hours going to five to ten shops, and one or two more hours with a detailed writing of the facts about every part of the visit.” Secret shoppers can make as much as £155 in one day for this work. They also get payment for fuel and sleep places, and money for the use of their car (they may go as far as 20,000 miles in a year). There are also camera secret shoppers, who make a record of the visit with a secret camera in a button or bag, who can get even more – about £300 for one day. The money they use in shops is normally given back to them as well.

“I am normally given between £5 and £20 to use at every shop, to make a record of the help I get from the person at the counter,” says Laura, a 50-year-old secret shopper from Devon, who has gone to about 7,000 shops since 2001. The thing she buys has to be a service or a sort of thing the shop owner needs her to look at. “I am always given a storyline, such as getting something from a certain part of the shop or new things, but inside this rule, I am often free to get any thing I desire – and keep it.”

Vienna is the best city in the world for living. Baghdad is the worst. London, Paris, and New York are not in the first 35, by an international test of quality of life. Cities where they say German are at the top of the list in the 18th Mercer Quality of Life test. Vienna is with Zurich, Munich, Dusseldorf, and Frankfurt in the first seven.

Paris has gone down the list, going ten places to number 37, a little before London at number 39, almost all because of the danger from violent attacks. The test looked at social and money conditions, health, teaching, houses, and the environment. Big business use it to see where they put offices and factories and how much to give workers.

Helena Hartlauer, who is 32 and was born in Vienna, said she was not surprised that her city is at the top. The government of Vienna has a long history of putting money into good houses for persons, so living in Vienna is almost the least hard among the great cities.

“I live in a room of 100 square meters in a good place, 20 minutes’ walk from the middle of the city. My payment for the room is only €800 every month.” A room like this in London would be more than £2,000, and even more in New York, which is number 44 on the list.

US cities do not do well in the test, mostly because of danger and crime. The best US city is San Francisco at number 28; Boston is number 34. Canadian cities, with Vancouver first, are much higher than US cities on the list.

“You do not see how safe Vienna is till you go to another country,” Hartlauer said. “We have very good public transport. The underground train is working all day and night at the end of the week, and it is only €1 for every journey.”

Clay Cockrell is in his office at Columbus Circle, across the road from 1 Central Park West, which is the building for Trump International Hotel and Tower. In front of the tower is Central Park, where Cockrell has his well-liked walk and talk help times. Cockrell, who was a Wall Street worker before he became a helper, goes through much of his day walking in Central Park or Battery Park in the lower part of Manhattan near Wall Street, as a friend and guide to some of the richest persons in New York. “I came to it in a natural way,” he said about becoming a knowledge man in help for persons with much money. “A number of those with great wealth – the 1% of the 1% – feel their troubles are not real troubles. But they are. Many helpers do not give enough value to their troubles.” So, what troubles are America’s 1% having? “There is pain over being rich at all,” he said. “There is the feeling that they have to keep the fact that they are rich a secret. And, there is being alone – being in the 1%, it seems, can make a person feel alone.” It seems F. Scott Fitzgerald was right: the very rich “are not the same as you and me.” Helpers say things have become worse after the money trouble and the talking about money division that was made greater by groups like Occupy Wall Street and the Fight for $15 good payment work. “The Occupy Wall Street group was a good one and had some important things to say about money division but it made the 1% different from others and made them in the eyes of all seem bad,” said Jamie Traeger-Muney, a help knowledge person and head of the Wealth Legacy Group. The news, she said, helps give the rich the feeling that “they need to keep secret or feel shame”. “Sometimes, I am surprised by the things persons say. You would never talk about another group of persons in the way it is seen as normal to talk about persons with much money.”

Swedish prisons have for a long time had a name in all places for being forward. But are prisons in the country an easy way? The head of the Sweden prison and probation group, Nils Oberg, said in November 2013 that four Swedish prisons will be shut because there are now much fewer prisoners than before. Even if there has been no fall in crime, between 2011 and 2012, the number of prisoners in Sweden went down by 6%, to a little over 4,500. A same drop is looked for in 2013 and 2014. Oberg said he was surprised by this fall, but he gave hope it was because of the way prisons are worked. "We hope that the work we do for making prisoners ready for a normal life and stopping them from doing crime again has helped," he said.

"The prison group in Sweden is much changed from when I first came as a young officer in 1978," says Kenneth Gustafsson, head of Kumla Prison, the most safe prison in Sweden. But he does not see the prisons as too soft. "When I started, the main idea was to give good care to prisoners. Prisoners got kind behaviour—maybe too kind, some would say. But after some well-known escapes in 2004, we had to make changes to keep prisons more safe."

Swedes still keep a wide human way for punishments, even for the most serious crimes: prison times are rarely more than ten years. Sweden was the first country in Europe to make use of electric tagging for those who have done wrong, and still tries to make jail times shorter when possible by using punishments in the town, which have been tested to give better results for stopping more crime. The reoffending number in Sweden is between 30 and 40% in three years; if we look at another country in Europe, the number is about twice as much in the UK.

A chief reason for the low number of people going back to crime and the small number of people in prison in Sweden (less than 70 for every 100,000 persons) is that the age where one is responsible for crime is 15. In the UK, for example, boys and girls from ten to 17 and young persons under 21 have the highest numbers of going back to crime: almost three out of four and two out of three, in turn. Many of these will go on to be in adult prisons. In Sweden, no young person under 21

A number of us have knowledge that we do not get enough sleep, but give thought to if we may put it right with a quite simple answer: getting up later. In a talk at the British Science meeting, Dr. Paul Kelley, a medical expert at the Sleep and Circadian Nerve Science organization at Oxford University, said schools may do well to have different starting times, to be in agreement with the natural body times of their students. This would make learning better, give better test outcomes, and make the health of students better (not enough sleep has been seen to be joined with disease such as sugar disease, feeling sad, too much fat, and weak body system against disease). This goes after a writing given in 2014, in which he said that, when boys and girls are about ten, the natural time for getting up is about 6.30 in the morning; at 16, this goes up to 8 in the morning; and, at 18, a young person who seems to be tired and slow has a natural time for waking up at 9 in the morning. The normal school starting time is good for boys and girls of ten but not for students from 16 to 18 years old. For the older students, it may be wiser to start the school day at 11 in the morning or later. "A 7 in the morning sound to get up for older young persons," Kelley and his group said in the writing, "is the same as a 4.30 in the morning start for a teacher of 50 years." He says it is not simple to make young persons go to bed earlier. "The body's natural time is controlled by a special sort of light," Kelley says. "The eye has more than only rods and cones; it has some cells that give news to the brain." This part of the brain has control of our day-and-night time over 24 hours. It is like saying: 'Why are you not able to have control of the rhythm of your heart?'"
